title: Alcaraz Out of French Open: Wrist Injury Sidelines Two-Time Champion
summary: The tennis world received a major shock on Friday when Carlos Alcaraz announced he will miss next month's French Open due to an ongoing wrist injury. The 22-year-old Spaniard, who has won the clay-court Grand Slam for the past two years, sustained the injury to his right wrist during his first-round match at the Barcelona Open last week. After undergoing tests, Alcaraz decided to withdraw from both the Italian Open and the French Open (Roland Garros). "After the results of the tests carried out today, we have decided that the most prudent thing to do is to be cautious and not participate in Rome or Roland Garros," Alcaraz posted on Instagram. "This is a difficult time for me, but I am sure we will come out of this stronger." The seven-time Grand Slam champion began 2026 by winning his first Australian Open title, becoming the youngest man to complete the career Grand Slam — winning each of the four major tournaments at least once. He lost the world number one ranking to long-time rival Jannik Sinner earlier this month after falling to him in the Monte Carlo final. Sinner, who has won four of the last nine Grand Slams, now has a golden opportunity to secure his own career Grand Slam, with the French Open being the only major missing from his collection. "It is very sad news," Sinner said after his win in Madrid. "Being that young like he is and like I am, we need to look at our bodies first before worse things happen. Hopefully he can be back for Wimbledon and we all hope for great battles in the future." Alcaraz has only missed one Grand Slam since qualifying for his first in 2021 — the 2023 Australian Open due to a hamstring injury.
why_it_matters: Alcaraz's injury reshapes the French Open field, giving other players a chance to win a major title. It also highlights how top athletes must balance peak performance with long-term health, a lesson young athletes can relate to in any sport.

Which Grand Slam title is missing from Jannik Sinner's collection?

background_read: Carlos Alcaraz (the 22-year-old Spanish tennis sensation) has been one of the most exciting players in the sport since breaking through in 2022. He is known for his explosive power, speed, and ability to win on all surfaces.

The French Open (Roland Garros) is the premier clay-court Grand Slam, and Alcaraz had won it in both 2024 and 2025, making him the defending champion. His absence opens the door for his rival Jannik Sinner (the Italian world No. 1), who has never won the French Open and is now a strong favorite.

Alcaraz has dealt with injuries before: he missed the 2023 Australian Open due to a hamstring problem. The current wrist issue occurred during the Barcelona Open, a traditional preparation event for the Monte Carlo and Madrid tournaments. The decision to withdraw from both the Italian Open and the French Open shows the team is prioritizing his long-term health.

The rivalry between Alcaraz and Sinner is one of the most compelling in modern tennis, with both players in their early 20s and already owning multiple Grand Slam titles. Their matches often draw huge audiences and are seen as the future of men's tennis.

perspectives.description: Alcaraz is making a mature decision to prioritize his long-term career over immediate glory. By skipping the French Open, he gives his wrist time to heal, which could prevent more serious damage. Fans can look forward to seeing him at Wimbledon and beyond, healthier than before.

Missing the French Open is a huge setback for Alcaraz, especially as the defending champion. There is no guarantee he will be 100% for Wimbledon, and the injury could affect his confidence and ranking. His absence also denies fans a potential epic showdown with Sinner on clay.

This situation underscores the demanding nature of the professional tennis calendar, where players often push their bodies to the limit. Withdrawals like this are not uncommon and often lead to a reshuffling of tournament favorites. The French Open now becomes more open, but also loses some star power.

The immediate focus for Alcaraz will be rehab and preparation for the grass-court season, with Wimbledon in July. If he returns strong, his rivalry with Sinner could deepen as both chase supremacy. For Sinner, this is a golden chance to complete his own career Grand Slam and cement his No.1 status.
